signature_writing_techniques.name: Deadpan action sting
quotes: Everybody is smiling. Nobody should be.
They do not drape. They stand.
Nobody won. Nobody says so.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: This writer repeatedly turns action lines into joke buttons by following a plain visual statement with a curt, corrective second sentence that lands like a vaudeville rimshot.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Very frequent; appears in most scenes, especially scene buttons and prop reveals.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Object-state escalation
quotes: Chocolate is on her apron, her cap, one ear. The slab around her station is a small chocolate accident.
Rice spills off the stovetop onto the counter. Off the counter onto the floor.
The apartment as we left it — laundry basket on the sofa, dust rag on the radio, the ironing board leaning against the wall.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: Comedy is built by tracking the changing condition of concrete objects and surfaces, not by abstract commentary or verbal cleverness.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Frequent in every set piece involving domestic labor, factory work, or aftermath.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Mechanical repetition loop
quotes: Pick up, roll, coat, set, swirl. Pick up, roll, coat, set, swirl.

Well, what do you do?

What kind of jobs do you have open?

Well, what do you do?

What kind of jobs do you have open?
Pick up, wrap, set down. Pick up, wrap, set down.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: The writer uses exact repetition as a comic machine, letting rhythm itself become the setup before failure breaks it.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Recurring at major comic engines; several times across the script, especially in workplace sequences.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Status undercut coda
quotes: Lucy and Ethel stand. Handshakes. Smiles. They walk out of the office with the bearing of two women who have just solved a problem that has not yet begun.
They stand in the living room — scorched blouse on the ironing board, rigid stockings in hand, the dinner plan settled. Complete confidence. Zero ability.
They smile. They say thank you. The smiles stay fixed and do not reach their eyes.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: Characters are often frozen in a pose of confidence or politeness that the action line immediately punctures, creating irony through status mismatch.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Common at scene endings and act-style buttons.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Domestic inventory framing
quotes: A fourth-floor walk-up on East 68th Street, 1952. Faded floral wallpaper. A sofa with cushions pressed into permanent shapes by the same two people every night.

Morning light through the window over the sink. A small kitchen — white tile counters, a four-burner gas stove, a Frigidaire humming in the corner, the checked tablecloth set for two, a percolator steaming on the counter.
The brownstone. Dusk. The fourth-floor windows lit amber. The city settling into night.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: Settings are introduced through practical household objects and wear patterns that double as class, routine, and comic context.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Strong in scene openings; appears in most new locations.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Mirrored couple beats
quotes:
FRED
(head snapping toward Ricky)
We will?

ETHEL
(head snapping toward Lucy)
We will?
The mirror image. Same alarm on Ethel’s face that was on Fred’s ten seconds ago. Two marriages. One pattern.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: The script treats the two couples as comic reflections, using repeated staging and echoed line shapes as part of the joke structure.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Distinctive but selective; concentrated in wager and reconciliation passages.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Compressed reaction beat
quotes: His face.
Nobody moves.
Nothing left to say.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: After a buildup, the writer often collapses the rhythm into a tiny standalone line that functions as both reaction shot and punchline.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Regularly deployed at peaks of embarrassment, panic, or scene closure.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Plainspoken ironic commentary
quotes: Four people in a living room. Two couples. One bet none of them can win.
A patient man. Not infinitely patient.
Not a question. She has put it together. Ricky does not deny it.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: The action lines occasionally step into concise interpretive commentary, but the commentary stays blunt, external, and comic rather than literary.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Moderate; appears every few scenes, usually to clarify comic logic.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Prop reveal reversal
quotes:
RICKY
It was the drugstore.

LUCY
The drugstore?

RICKY
I left my hat.

LUCY
When you picked up these two breakfast orders.
Lucy takes her box. Looks at it. Five pounds of chocolate.
Ricky picks up a pair of stockings from the laundry basket. Holds them up. They are rigid — starched stiff, pressed with creases sharp enough to cut paper.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: A concrete object is withheld or recontextualized until the last possible beat, and the reveal itself carries the laugh.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Frequent in domestic and factory gag construction.

signature_writing_techniques.name: Parallel closure lines
quotes: The same room where the bet was made. The same sofa, the same armchair, the same lamp light.
They smile. They say thank you.
The lights in the fourth-floor window go out, one by one.
signature_writing_techniques.why_this_author: The writer likes short parallel declaratives at the end of scenes to create symmetry, closure, and a final visual hold.
signature_writing_techniques.frequency: Most visible in the final section and other scene exits; moderate overall.

sentence_level_patterns.average_sentence_length: Action-line average is about 11.8 words per line across 219 lines, but the felt sentence rhythm alternates between 1-5 word beats and 13-25 word setup lines.
sentence_level_patterns.cadence_variation: Highly contrastive cadence: descriptive inventory or process line, then a clipped corrective fragment or reaction beat. The writer likes punch-then-breathe and breathe-then-snap alternation rather than uniform flow.
sentence_level_patterns.clause_structure_preference: Prefers short declaratives, fragments, and simple coordination over nested subordination. When clauses extend, they usually remain concrete and list-based rather than syntactically ornate.
sentence_level_patterns.punctuation_habits: Heavy period use, moderate comma use, zero semicolons, zero ellipses, and recurring em dashes for interruption or appositive comic correction. Question marks and exclamation marks cluster in dialogue, not narration.
demonstrative_quotes: A patient man. Not infinitely patient.
Snodgrass closes his eyes. Opens them.
Lucy walks to the kitchen door. Puts her hand on the knob. Pushes it open. Looks inside.
Four people in a living room. Two couples. One bet none of them can win.
The beautiful breakfast sits on the table, half-eaten, fully fraudulent.

vocabulary_clusters: wear, residue, spill, and mess vocabulary
diction_fingerprint.register_and_formality: Action lines are plain, visual, and production-facing with occasional dry omniscient wit. Dialogue is colloquial, mid-century domestic speech: simple idiom, contractions, direct address, and joke logic over polish.
diction_fingerprint.word_frequency_patterns: The writer favors short Anglo-Saxon words and concrete nouns: room, floor, door, box, hat, blouse, stockings, rice, chocolate, belt, kitchen. Recurrent evaluative words include same, nobody, confidence, good, over, ready, and mess. The script avoids abstract emotional vocabulary and prestige-drama lyricism.
distinctive_diction_quotes: A sofa with cushions pressed into permanent shapes by the same two people every night.
The room smells like floor wax and other people’s cigarettes.
The result does not resemble a swirl. It resembles a fingerprint at a crime scene.
They move like women who have been on their feet for eight hours doing something they should never have attempted.
The smiles stay fixed and do not reach their eyes.

dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.speech_rhythm: Quick, reactive, short-burst dialogue with frequent pivots from confidence to bluff to alarm. She often answers before she has evidence, then doubles down or jokes her way through the gap.
verbal_tics_or_recurring_phrases: What kind of jobs do you have open?
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.emotional_range_in_dialogue: When defensive, she wisecracks. When cornered, she repeats or improvises. When frightened, her lines shorten into direct appeals. When conciliatory, she stays plain and self-deprecating rather than sentimental.
distinguishing_markers: Turns accusation into a joke instead of an apology
Uses enthusiasm as camouflage for uncertainty
Escalates from literal answer to dare or bluff
Frequently recruits others into action with direct address
Keeps vocabulary simple even when lying
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.signature_line: That’s why they call them tellers — they go around blabbing everything they know.

dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.speech_rhythm: Short-to-medium speeches, often question-led and prosecutorial at first, then excuse-making, then command-heavy under stress. He carries some of the script’s longest conciliatory speeches.
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.emotional_range_in_dialogue: Angry Ricky repeats and presses with questions. Embarrassed Ricky spins explanations. Panicked Ricky barks imperatives. Tender Ricky shifts into collective language and negotiated reset.
distinguishing_markers: Uses rhetorical questioning as pressure
Alternates warmth and correction in the same exchange
Frames conflict in terms of proof, terms, or practical arrangement
Shifts fastest into shouted command mode during disaster
Uses colloquial masculine filler and easy contractions
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.signature_line: Lucy. Now look. I’m serious. I don’t know what’s the matter with you. Every month — every single month — your bank account is overdrawn.

dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.speech_rhythm: Dry, grumbly, declarative, and slightly slower than Ricky. He delivers broad pronouncements and deadpan practical nonsense with total confidence.
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.emotional_range_in_dialogue: When smug, he generalizes. When surprised, he sputters briefly. When panicked, he still sounds literal-minded. When reconciled, he stays blunt and unornamented.
distinguishing_markers: Frames opinions as broad social truths
Uses old-fashioned masculine idiom
Occasional nonstandard grammar
Treats absurd domestic mistakes as ordinary know-how
Deadpan confidence even when obviously wrong
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.signature_line: Let’s face it, Rick. When it comes to money, there are two kinds of people — the earners and the spenders. Or as they are more popularly known, husbands and wives.

dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.speech_rhythm: Compact, practical, and reactive. She often echoes Lucy with less conviction, then grounds the scene with a plainspoken concern or endorsement.
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.emotional_range_in_dialogue: She admits fear directly, resists in short practical terms, and accepts resolution simply. Her lines stay shorter and less theatrical than Lucy’s across moods.
distinguishing_markers: Directly names practical worry instead of joking around it
Supports Lucy’s lies with weaker conviction
Uses grounded bodily or household concerns
Less flourish and less verbal speed than Lucy
Often functions as the plainspoken echo or check
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.signature_line: We’ll change places. We’ll get jobs, and you take care of the house for a week.

dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.speech_rhythm: Procedural office cadence that shortens into clipped frustration. He repeats the same question structure until patience breaks.
verbal_tics_or_recurring_phrases: Let’s be honest with one another
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.emotional_range_in_dialogue: Starts formal and patient, then becomes irritated without losing his occupational structure. Even his frustration remains procedural.
distinguishing_markers: Occupational vocabulary and list structures
Exact repetition of the same question
Formal tone amid comic chaos
Longer explanatory office sentences than the domestic cast
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.signature_line: Look, ladies, let’s be honest with one another. I can’t find you a job until you can tell me what you can really do.

dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.speech_rhythm: Short supervisory bursts, command-first, humorless, and rhythm-setting. Her lines are functional triggers for the machinery of the gag.
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.emotional_range_in_dialogue: Narrow by design: stern, procedural, unimpressed, briefly approving only when output appears correct. She does not banter.
distinguishing_markers: Institutional vocabulary
Imperatives over conversation
Minimal contractions in key commands
No joke participation
Authority lines function as mechanical cues
dialogue_fingerprint_per_character.signature_line: There’s no room in this plant for levity, however weak.

tension.technique: Tension is built through compression, repetition, and visible process pressure rather than interior dread; the line turns an object into a countdown.

humor.quote: Ricky picks up a pair of stockings from the laundry basket. Holds them up. They are rigid — starched stiff, pressed with creases sharp enough to cut paper. They do not drape. They stand.
humor.technique: Humor comes from plain visual reporting plus one concrete exaggeration, then a blunt final sentence that refuses to oversell the gag.

grief.register: ABSENT as true grief; closest equivalent is humiliation and surrender
grief.technique: The script does not render grief; emotional low points are handled as curt mutual defeat rather than sorrow.

wonder.register: Largely absent; only mild atmospheric composure
wonder.quote: The brownstone. Dusk. The fourth-floor windows lit amber. The city settling into night.
wonder.technique: When the script pauses for visual appreciation, it stays restrained and practical, using short exterior fragments rather than awe language.

fear.quote:
Catch the rice! Hurry up, Fred!
...
Hurry up, will you?! It’s still coming!
fear.technique: Fear is rendered through imperative bursts, repetition, and accelerating practical commands, not named emotion.

violence.technique: Violence is sudden, broad, and consequence-light; the page gives the impact in one emphatic visual beat, then tracks the mess.

intimacy.quote: Listen, we don’t know how you girls feel about it, but we’d like to forget the whole thing.
intimacy.technique: Intimacy is dialogue-driven and practical, expressed through concession, shared space, and group tableau rather than romantic language.

quotes: Not running — walking with purpose.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Across all chapter fragments, the script uses standard transitions like "FADE IN:", "CUT TO:", "SMASH CUT TO:", and "FADE TO BLACK," but no "ANGLE ON," "CLOSE ON," "PAN," "TRACKING," or lens language.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI often reaches for camera jargon to simulate screenplay authority, especially in visual comedy, but this writer stages emphasis through blocking and line order instead.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Fragments repeatedly note no V.O.; one chapter has only a single "FRED (O.S.)" cue, and no narration track carries exposition.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI uses V.O. or explanatory narration to patch clarity gaps, but this writer keeps information in live dialogue and visible action.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Emotion is inferred from behavior: "Lucy stops buttering." "Snodgrass closes his eyes. Opens them." "The smiles stay fixed and do not reach their eyes." No thought-stream access appears.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI defaults to naming thoughts and realizations because it is easier than staging behavior, but this writer stays filmable.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Even repetitive labor sequences are written linearly; fragments explicitly note no montage blocks or montage syntax despite ideal opportunities.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI often compresses repeated action into MONTAGE shorthand, but this writer wants the repetition itself to generate comic rhythm.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Comparisons are sparse and concrete: "a fingerprint at a crime scene," "creases sharp enough to cut paper." There is no sustained poetic imagery field.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI often over-poeticizes scene description to sound 'writerly,' but this writer uses metaphor only as a punchline tool.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Observed parentheticals are functional: "(head snapping toward Ricky)," "(leaning in)," "(to herself)," "(looking inside)." No "(sadly)," "(angrily)," or similar emotion labels appear.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI often uses emotional parentheticals as shortcuts for performance, but this writer prefers action and dialogue to carry feeling.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Summed punctuation counts across all fragments show ellipses at 0.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI uses ellipses to imply hesitation or mood, but this writer uses line breaks, em dashes, or clipped statements instead.

negative_space_map.absence_evidence: Summed punctuation counts across all fragments show semicolons at 0.
negative_space_map.why_ai_defaults: AI often inserts semicolons to sound polished or literary, but this writer's rhythm depends on periods, fragments, and simple coordination.

show_explain_ratio.show_language_description: Very high show ratio. The script externalizes through props, surfaces, body placement, repetitive labor verbs, room inventory, and reaction beats: hats, aprons, stockings, rice, chocolate, doors, floors, windows, belts.
show_explain_ratio.explain_language_description: Explain language appears only in short comic clarifiers such as "Not a question," "Nobody won," or "One pattern." Even these remain external and scene-serving rather than interpretive essays.
show_explain_ratio.approximate_balance: Roughly 85-90% show / 10-15% explain in action lines, with the explain portion mostly blunt comic framing rather than emotional abstraction.
show_explain_ratio.enforcement_note: Generated prose must stay ruthlessly filmable. If a passage starts naming feelings, lessons, or meanings more often than it names objects, gestures, and visible mess, the voice has drifted.

comparative_exclusion.overlapping_quality: Domestic comedy built from argument rhythm and clean joke logic.
differentiating_techniques: This writer relies more heavily on prop-state escalation and visual slapstick residue.
Action lines are blunter and more production-practical, with less verbal polish in the jokes themselves.

comparative_exclusion.overlapping_quality: Mid-century sitcom compression and ensemble reaction timing.
differentiating_techniques: This writer uses more mirrored-couple structure and mechanical repetition loops.
The descriptive voice is more inventory-based and less conversationally essayed.

comparative_exclusion.overlapping_quality: Deadpan reversals and efficient scene buttons.
differentiating_techniques: This writer is less cynical and less verbally barbed, with broader domestic slapstick.
The action lines stay more room-and-prop grounded and less urbane in diction.

action_line_metrics.average_words_per_line: 11.2 words per action line overall (2460 words / 219 lines). Range runs from 1-3 word beats to 26+ word setup lines, with the center of gravity in 9-18 words.
action_line_metrics.fragment_percentage: 25.6% by aggregated fragment-line count (56 / 219), with a stronger felt fragment presence because many non-fragment lines are themselves very short declaratives.
action_line_metrics.verb_first_percentage: 13.2% (29 / 219). Verb-first openings are present but secondary to article and character-name openings.
action_line_metrics.all_caps_density: Sparse and functional. ALL CAPS is used for character cues, occasional sound/object emphasis like "A KNOCK," and rare event emphasis like "EXPLODES" or "SCREAMS," not as a constant stylistic field.
action_line_metrics.paragraph_rhythm: Alternates between clustered descriptive setup blocks and isolated short reaction lines. The writer does not put every sentence on its own line; emphasis comes from selective isolation after fuller inventory or process description.
demonstrative_quotes: A fourth-floor walk-up on East 68th Street, 1952. Faded floral wallpaper. A sofa with cushions pressed into permanent shapes by the same two people every night.
Snodgrass closes his eyes. Opens them.
Lucy watches the woman beside her. Pick up, roll, coat, set, swirl. Pick up, roll, coat, set, swirl. It looks easy.
Lucy walks to the kitchen door. Puts her hand on the knob. Pushes it open. Looks inside.

screenplay_structure_metrics.scene_density: Approximately 4-5 scenes across 5 pages in the supplied material, or about 0.8-1.0 scenes per page; scenes are compact but not hyper-cut.
screenplay_structure_metrics.int_ext_ratio: Interior-heavy, approximately 3:1 INT to EXT based on observed headings and scene descriptions.
screenplay_structure_metrics.action_to_dialogue_ratio: Action slightly outweighs dialogue by word count: about 2460 action words to 1423 dialogue words, roughly 1.73:1.
screenplay_structure_metrics.transition_types: Mostly hard cuts via new scene headings, with occasional explicit "CUT TO:", "SMASH CUT TO:", and "FADE TO" variants. Transition vocabulary is minimal and standard.
screenplay_structure_metrics.parenthetical_vocabulary: Used sparingly and functionally: "(head snapping toward Ricky)," "(leaning in)," "(to herself)," "(looking inside)," "(calling toward the production room)." Never observed: emotional adverbs, literary performance notes, or motivational commentary.
screenplay_structure_metrics.character_introduction_patterns: Character cues are standard uppercase names. Introductions tend to be name plus immediate visual/action context rather than age blocks or elaborate physical biographies.

a pressure cooker on the back burner, hissing faintly

a clipboard against her ribs like a weapon
That one landed.

exclamation_marks_narration.sample_size: Observed punctuation counts are aggregated, but exclamations overwhelmingly cluster in dialogue; narration examples show near-zero use except ALL CAPS event emphasis.

em_dashes.sample_size: 60 em dashes across full supplied source

question_marks_narration.sample_size: No narration/action-line question marks evidenced across supplied fragments

period_to_comma_ratio.sample_size: 344 periods / 209 commas across full supplied source

rhythm_change_frequency.sample_size: 218 consecutive transitions across full supplied source

rhythm_transition_architecture.rhythm_change_frequency: 61.5% of consecutive action lines change category (134 changes / 218 transitions).
rhythm_transition_architecture.max_consecutive_same_category: Long lines can cluster most heavily; observed same-category ceiling is at least 21 consecutive long-to-long transitions in one chapter-level count cluster. Runtime should avoid more than 22 consecutive long-category lines.
signature_moves: After a short line (4-6w), this writer expands to a long line 64.8% of the time. The runtime should replicate this setup-then-expansion move.
After a medium line (7-12w), the next line goes long 50.8% of the time, showing a tendency to build outward before snapping back.
Ultra-short beats rarely stay stacked; after an ultra-short line, 84.6% of the time the writer moves to short, medium, or long rather than repeating another ultra-short.
anti_patterns: Short-to-short repetition is extremely rare at 1.9%; avoid strings of evenly clipped 4-6 word lines.
Medium-to-medium repetition is also rare at 10.2%; this writer does not coast in a flat middle rhythm for long.
Do not stack long runs of ultra-short beats; the source uses them as punctuation, not as the dominant texture.

beat_architecture_protocol.beat_frequency: 6.4% of action lines by aggregated beat count (14 beats / 219 action lines), with additional beat-like short lines beyond the strict counted set.
beat_architecture_protocol.beat_placement: Beats appear after dialogue volleys, at the instant of realization, before tonal resets, and at scene endings. They often follow a buildup of process or argument and serve as the final comic punctuation mark.
beat_architecture_protocol.beat_density_by_context: Beats cluster most in panic/comic escalation scenes and reconciliation pivots, with a secondary use in exterior or scene-opening transitions. They are less common in pure setup description.

scene_transition_compression_protocol.closing_line_avg_length: 7.6 words per closing action line (114 words / 15 closing lines), notably shorter than the overall 11.2-word action-line average.
closing_line_examples: Everybody is smiling. Nobody should be.
We hold on the building for a breath.
The beautiful breakfast sits on the table, half-eaten, fully fraudulent.
Lucy and Ethel stand. Handshakes. Smiles. They walk out of the office with the bearing of two women who have just solved a problem that has not yet begun.
Roll, coat, set, swirl. She has not looked up once.
The lights in the fourth-floor window go out, one by one.
scene_transition_compression_protocol.transition_guidance: End scenes on a compressed visual state, reaction beat, or ironic status line. Closing lines should usually be shorter than the scene’s average and should not drift into reflective explanation. Prefer one image, one blunt judgment, or one tiny freeze-frame beat.

element_rules.prose_translation_rule: Translate into compressed narrative sentences that preserve concrete props, bodily placement, and joke timing. Do not expand into novelistic atmosphere or interior monologue.
Translate into a single establishing sentence or a white-space scene break. Keep location framing brief and practical.
Translate as paragraph or scene breaks. SMASH CUT should feel abrupt with no bridge; FADE may allow one soft visual bridge sentence.
Convert into a minimal physical cue, delivery modifier, or glance. Never translate parentheticals into narrator commentary about emotion.
Translate into light attribution only when needed. Preserve the character’s documented speech rhythm rather than over-tagging.
Translate into sentence isolation, typographic emphasis per house style, or blunt declarative force. Do not convert to excited narration or extra exclamation marks.
Translate into a tiny physical pause or reaction action such as a look, stop, or held object. Do not write abstract silence language.

quantitative_translation_mappings.rationale: Prose needs slightly more connective tissue than screenplay action lines, but fragments remain a core signature and must stay visibly present.
Prose sentences can run slightly longer than action lines, lowering period density somewhat, but the voice still depends on frequent stops.
A small prose allowance is acceptable for readability, but this writer’s relative comma restraint must remain intact.
Narrative prose can absorb a little more continuity, but once sentences regularly exceed mid-teens length the voice loses its sitcom compression.
The source never opens action lines with participles; prose must preserve that avoidance because AI overuses this construction.
Verb-first openings contribute briskness, but they are a secondary texture, not the dominant opener pattern.

quantitative_translation_mappings.source_value: Lucy 13, Ricky 15, Fred 14, Ethel 13, Snodgrass 14, Forelady 17
quantitative_translation_mappings.drift_ceiling: hard ceiling at source max for each character
quantitative_translation_mappings.rationale: Dialogue should never become more verbose in prose than it is on the page; the screenplay already defines the upper bound of each voice.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not replace prop-based visual comedy with explanatory jokes or meta commentary.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: "They do not drape. They stand." and "The beautiful breakfast sits on the table, half-eaten, fully fraudulent." let objects carry the laugh.
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Build a concrete prop, reveal its wrong state visually, then cap it with one short reaction or deadpan action line.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not write modern snark, irony-saturated banter, or self-aware sitcom dialogue.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: Dialogue stays plain and period-colloquial: "Gee," "Yeah," "What kind of jobs do you have open?" and "We’re lousy housewives."
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Use simple colloquial speech, earnest overconfidence, repetition, and literal misunderstanding.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not add camera jargon or shot-design micromanagement.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: Across the supplied source, transitions remain basic while no "CLOSE ON," "ANGLE," or "PAN" language appears.
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Stage emphasis through blocking, reaction beats, and line sequencing.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not expand action lines into lush, prestige-drama prose detached from comic function.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: Even richer description stays practical: "A sofa with cushions pressed into permanent shapes by the same two people every night."
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Use specific domestic or workplace details that reveal routine, class, and comic wear in plain language.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not render emotion through interior monologue or cognitive-verb explanation.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: The script uses visible behavior instead: "Lucy stops buttering." "Snodgrass closes his eyes. Opens them." "The smiles stay fixed and do not reach their eyes."
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Show feeling through gesture, posture, object handling, and clipped reaction lines.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not replace physical escalation with commentary-heavy banter.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: "Rice spills off the stovetop onto the counter. Off the counter onto the floor." and "piece by piece, becoming human storage" build comedy through accumulation.
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Escalate by spreading, piling, sticking, overflowing, or deforming concrete objects while dialogue stays short and reactive.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not overuse evenly clipped short lines for long stretches.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: The rhythm matrix shows short-to-short transitions at only 1.9%, while the writer usually expands from short to long.
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: After a short beat, open back out into a medium or long descriptive line.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not use -ing participle openings in narration.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: Aggregated metric count shows 0 -ing openings across 219 action lines.
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Open with articles, character names, pronouns, or occasional verb-first declaratives.

ip_specific_bans.ban: Do not moralize the ending with explicit lesson statements.
ip_specific_bans.evidence: Resolution is carried by objects and blunt closure lines: "Nobody won. Nobody says so." and the chocolate-box reveal.
ip_specific_bans.positive_replacement: Let a room image, prop payoff, or curt declarative line embody the conclusion.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if no universal or IP-specific banned token, mold, or technique appears. Any camera jargon, modern snark, semicolon, ellipsis, or -ing opener fails immediately.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Scan generated prose for banned vocabulary, punctuation, sentence molds, camera terms, and IP-specific anti-pattern phrases.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Regenerate the offending passage from concrete action and prop logic rather than patching individual words.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if all emotional and cognitive states are filmable. Any "realized," "noticed that," "found herself," or similar separation verb in narration fails.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Search for cognitive-verb constructions and test whether the sentence could be photographed without voice-over.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Remove the cognition verb and replace it with visible behavior, object handling, or a reaction beat.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if the narrator never comments on the story’s meaning, structure, or moral significance. Any thesis-like sentence fails.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Flag abstract commentary after concrete images and any sentence that sounds like interpretation rather than staging.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Cut the commentary. End on the image, prop state, or blunt status line instead.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if sentence openers roughly match the documented distribution, with article and character-name openings dominant, verb-first secondary, and zero -ing openings.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Count opener types across the passage and compare against the enforcement ranges.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Redistribute openings toward articles, names, and pronouns; remove participial starts.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if narrative sentences stay in the documented compression band and avoid bloated descriptive paragraphs.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Measure average sentence length, period density, and paragraph block size. Flag passages where average sentence length exceeds 16 words or where long reflective blocks dominate.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Cut adjectives, split sentences, restore fragments, and move emphasis into isolated short lines.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if the passage shows the writer’s expansion-and-snap rhythm rather than flat uniform pacing.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Check line/sentence length sequence against the transition architecture. Flag excessive short-to-short repetition or long unbroken middle-range runs.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Insert a compressed beat after buildup or expand a short line into a longer inventory line, matching the documented transition tendencies.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if each character’s speeches stay within their documented AVG/P90/P95/MAX range and preserve contraction density.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Measure speech lengths per character and compare against character ceilings. Flag over-complete, essay-like dialogue.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Shorten speeches, restore contractions, and break explanation into volleys or reactions.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if Lucy, Ricky, Fred, Ethel, and authority figures remain immediately distinguishable by rhythm and vocabulary.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Read each character’s lines without attribution and compare against their fingerprint markers.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Rewrite the blurring voice using the character’s documented tics, sentence length, and conflict style.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if major comic beats are carried by visible objects, surfaces, clothing, food, or room condition rather than by explanation alone.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Identify whether each comic beat has a concrete prop anchor. If the joke exists only in commentary, it fails.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Add or foreground a household/workplace object in the wrong state, then let reaction complete the gag.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if settings are grounded in practical room details and not generic atmosphere.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Scan for concrete nouns tied to furniture, appliances, clothing, food, or surfaces. Flag vague room description.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Replace generic setting language with 2-4 specific domestic or workplace details that reveal use and wear.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if screenplay-derived delivery cues become physical action or minimal attribution, not emotional narration.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Check translated dialogue tags and interline narration for adverbial emotion labels or over-tagging.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Convert the cue into a glance, lean, stop, hand movement, or object interaction.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if documented collocations are preserved where relevant and not replaced by generic AI synonyms.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Compare passages involving domestic mess, confidence, reaction, or room detail against the collocation list.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Swap generic substitutions for the writer’s exact pairings when the same semantic slot appears.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if all ABSOLUTE and HIGH confidence constraints are met: zero semicolons, zero ellipses, zero narration question marks, zero -ing openings, and punctuation/rhythm metrics within range.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Run deterministic metric checks on punctuation density, opener distribution, fragment rate, and rhythm change frequency.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: If any ABSOLUTE or HIGH constraint fails, reject and regenerate the passage from scratch.

fourteen_point_audit_protocol.pass_fail_definition: Pass only if scene endings compress into a visual image, status line, dialogue-adjacent reaction, or beat shorter than the passage average.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.detection_method: Inspect the final 1-3 sentences of each scene or passage. Flag reflective wrap-ups or overlong closing sentences.
fourteen_point_audit_protocol.repair_instruction: Replace the ending with one image, one blunt declarative, or one tiny reaction beat in the documented closing style.

voice_decay_prevention_protocol.re_anchoring_trigger: Every 300-400 words of generated prose, re-inject the Numerical Enforcement Layer, top 5 signature techniques, punctuation profile, and rhythm transition summary.
passage_level_enforcement_checks: Reject if any ellipsis appears in narration or dialogue.
Reject if any narration question mark appears.
Reject if any -ing participle opening appears in narration.
Check period density against 11.5-13.5 per 100 prose words target.
Check comma density against 8.0-9.5 per 100 prose words target and 10.5 ceiling.
Check fragment rate against 18-24% prose target and 16% floor.
Check opener distribution for article/name/pronoun dominance and zero-to-near-zero -ing openings.
Check no character speech exceeds documented hard ceiling.
Check no three consecutive same-pronoun sentence openings.
Check scene endings compress into image, status, dialogue-adjacent reaction, or beat.
Check at least one concrete prop or room-state anchor exists in each comic beat.
drift_detection_metrics: Fragment rate trending downward across 3+ passages
Period density trending downward across 3+ passages
Comma density trending upward across 3+ passages
Verb-first openings trending upward above ceiling or collapsing below floor
Dialogue speeches trending longer across 3+ passages
Increase in abstract emotional vocabulary relative to concrete nouns
Loss of compressed scene buttons at passage endings
